original_abstract: Abstract Digital twin (DT) technology has received immense attention over the years due to the promises it presents to various stakeholders in science and engineering. As a result, different thematic areas of DT have been explored. This is no different in specific fields such as manufacturing, automation, oil and gas, and civil engineering, leading to fragmented approaches for field-specific applications. The civil engineering industry is further disadvantaged in this regard as it relies on external techniques by other engineering fields for its DT adoption. A rising consequence of these extensions is a concentrated application of DT to the operations and maintenance phase. On another spectrum, building information modeling (BIM) is pervasively utilized in the planning/design phase, and the transient nature of the construction phase remains a challenge for its DT adoption. In this article, we present a phase-based development of DT in the architecture, engineering, and construction industry. We commence by presenting succinct expositions on DT as a concept and as a service, and establish a five-level scale system. Furthermore, we present separately a systematic literature review of the conventional techniques employed at each civil engineering phase. In this regard, we identified enabling technologies such as computer vision for extended sensing and the Internet of things for reliable integration. Ultimately, we attempt to reveal DT as an important tool across the entire life cycle of civil engineering projects, and nudge researchers to think more holistically in their quest for the integration of DT for civil engineering applications.
humanized_abstract: As more manufacturers begin experimenting with the use of Digital twin (DT) technology in the industry, the use of Abstract DT is increasingly becoming a buzzword in science and engineering. Indeed, a number of DT application domains have been explored. In a number of specific domains such as manufacturing, automation, oil and gas and civil engineering, the use of DTs remain fragmented and thus have not yielded any tangible and directly related field-of-practice benefits. Specifically, the civil engineering sector relies on a number of extension services provided by other disciplines, which can limit the take-up of DT technologies, with the majority of its use cases restricted to the Operations and Maintenance phase, while other phases are inadequately supported. Conversely, Building information modeling (BIM) is increasingly being used in the Design phase, while the build/construction phase is considered a dynamic and complex period, which makes difficult its adoption as a phase within a DT technology. In this paper, we are concerned with developing the Digital twin technology along the civil engineering phases from conceptualization to completion of projects, by providing an introduction of DT as a concept as well as the associated DT-as-a-Service at the onset. Furthermore, a five-level categorisation framework is given, which forms a precursor to carrying out a systematic review for each of the five stages that make up the civil engineering projects in order to identify the current challenges that can be addressed through use of Computer vision technologies for Extended Sensing as well as Internet of Things technologies for reliable integration of assets to enhance adoption of DT. Thus, the overarching goal of this research work is to highlight the DT applications across the whole life cycle of infrastructure projects in order to steer future works toward a more holistic and overarching view for DT in civil engineering applications.
